Is (international) law capable of regulating social media? 61 Summary of findings 64 List of references 65 Introduction The proliferation of the Internet along with the rise of social networking has changed the modern contact scenario dramatically. While the pre-internet age was largely confined to one-way distribution of information, the present era is characterized by two-way or even multi-way user-friendly interaction, which is cheap, or even free, and fast. The changing role of the Internet in how people acquire knowledge, engage in interpersonal contact and communicate their thoughts, opinions or views , and express their identities,  has been pointed out by many studies around the world. Social networking is a strong tool with approximately 3.499 billion active users as of 2019, with an average of 7.6 social media accounts for each user . Governments often argue the need to regulate social media because of the abuses of the right to free speech.  Part of the challenge with regulatory  approaches  to social media platforms like Facebook  and  VKontakte  results from their dual role as public and private  domains . The se  platforms have become an important part of contemporary public  life,   as t hey facilitate our social infrastructure and have been compared with public utilities. Yet, as private services they are governed by commercial priorities and shareholder interests rather than the public interest.  T he se  companies’ private control over a widely used public resource and their ability to turn all  types  of human activity into  market  data has  helped to create  the  reaches companies on   the global market .  On the other  hand  the standard approach to freedom of dissemination of information as a political liberty is unhelpful given the importance of social networks for private and family life of many individuals. The overall goal of the present research is to understand the effectiveness of the existing international human rights standards as applied to social media. Against this goal the research focuses on the legal implications of social media, the scope of the right to disseminate information and freedom of expression in the realities of social networks, international legal standards for restrictions on the operation of social  media, the positive obligations of states with regard to freedom of expression on social networks. These are the main research questions addressed in the three chapters of the paper. In the first chapter is a given a brief definition of Social Media platforms as a legal phenomenon. By presenting a series of features and legal issues that arise from them. The author has also provided a comparison of the legal framework or legal regulation initiatives aimed at regulating the functioning of social networks at national, international and regional level. In this way a clearer picture is given of the different approaches regarding the regulation of social networks.    The second chapter presents a detailed  analysis  of legal issues  related to  social networks, from content moderation, change of approach to some basic concepts such as the right to privacy in social networks, and the conflict of fundamental rights in the report.  The third chapter presents an analysis of the legal challenges for regulating social media by means of international law. Chapter 1. Social media platforms as a legal phenomenon Social media platforms defined: legally relevant characteristics of social media platforms The proliferation of the  Internet   along with  the rise of social networking  has  changed the modern contact scenario dramatically. While the pre-internet age was largely confined to one-way distribution of information, the present era is characterized by two-way  or even multi-way  user-friendly interaction, which is cheap ,  or  even  free ,  and fast. The changing role of the  Internet  in how people acquire knowledge, engage in interpersonal contact and communicate their thoughts, opinions or views has been pointed out by  many  studies around the world. Social networking is a strong tool with approximately 3.499 billion active users as of 2019, with an average of 7.6 social media accounts for each user .  Social networking platforms are increasingly and furiously growing, becoming indispensable to customers. Facebook surpassed Google as the number one site where internet users spend much of their online time in August 2010, for the first time - 41 million hours for Facebook users versus 39.9 million hours for Google.  A recent Nielsen study found that overall, people use social media apps to spend a quarter of their online time. And  it is  not just  younger people : a Pew Research study released in August 2010 found that the usage of social media sites such as Facebook and  LinkedIn  by adults aged fifty to sixty-four between April 2009 and May 2010 increased by a whopping 88 percent. This research focuses on the new Web 2.0 technology. For web-based applications such as blogs, wikis, social networking, and media-sharing sites, the  word Web 2.0 is an umbrella term. The first to coin the word 'Web 2.0' was Tim O'Reilly. Web 2.0 laid the groundwork for Social Network Services growth. The concept of platforms of social media is both  broad  and mercurial. Some definitions define social media platforms as apps that allow "user-generated content" (Kaplan &  Haenlein ).  Other meanings concentrate on the ability to share data with distributed groups of recipients interactively (Hogan & Quan- Haase ). Another scholar defines  ‘ online social  networking’  sites as websites, the primary aim of which is to serve as a connection between users to create online social networks through the use of computer software.  Boyd and Ellison define social network sites as "web-based services that allow individuals within a bounded framework to create a public or semi-public profile, articulate a list of other users with whom they share a link, and display and traverse their list of links and those created within the system by others.  This definition is widely accepted by scholars .   It is true that the essence and intent of each  social  media can differ from site to site.  Grimmelmann  points out that the concept of Boyd and Ellison highlights three significant aspects of online  Social  networks: identity creation; relationships; and community . As  it was  mentioned before, social media services are Web 2.0 Internet-based applications.  The World Wide Web was mainly a platform for consumption before Web 2.0 applications became popular.  Using Internet in the late 1990s  mostly  consiste d  on spending much of the time on the internet reading what other people had written and watching audio and video that were mostly present  in commercial media.  As Web 2.0 technologies have made the Internet more interactive, Web 2.0 applications have modified the way we communicate with the online environment and the other people we engage with through it. However, the transition to Web 2.0 was not simply the outcome of a substantial change in technology, but rather in ideology.   The change to Web 2.0 can be defined as a shift from the user as a consumer to the user as a participant. Some have implied that the user's new position is basically "prosumer," a mix of consumer and producer.   Applications are designed to allow people to make, communicate, collaborate and exchange content development and consumption processes. Web 2.0 has served as a platform for content creation and publication for more than a decade now, as well as a space where content can be "continuously modified in a participatory and collaborative way by all users.”   Content created by users is the essence of social media.   The ideology is Web 2.0 and user-generated content is the fuel. "For that matter, the personal details  users  enter    into  their  Facebook profile or other social media profile, the network relation  they  create by "friend," the video  users  upload on YouTube, the message  they  share on Twitter, the blog entry  they  posted, the comment  they  write on that blog entry, the images  they  add to Instagram, the pictures  they  upload,  their  “like” of someone’s picture or comme n t, and an enormous number of other user-generated decisions that populate social media sites across the internet and ultimately connect us together are examples of user-generated content that fuels social networking sites. Without this  content, without our choices and behavior, social networking platforms w ould  be, what MySpace  was  changing into, a ghost town, a vast, vibrant midway packed with games that no   one plays.   The phenomenon of  u ser-generated content has brought many advantages to  society,  however, it has also brought many legal challenges and concerns. Such challenges are widespread and are seen from the multiplicity of news  reports and cases that highlight different questionable behaviors and practices of users. Evidently, some of these practices are illegal, others border on legality, yet others are undesirable or morally indefensible. These generated user-content raise s  a question of legal ownership  and liability  over the content .  On one side is the user who creates the content , the fuel for the social network platform and on the other side is the platform which without it the user is not enable to practice its right as User-Generated Content creator . People and communities develop user-specific accounts for a site or app built and managed by a social media provider.  The user profile is the core of the Social Media service.  As well as the choices for identifying oneself, the type of identifying information required varies considerably from service to service, but also involves the prospect of generating a username, supplying contact information and uploading a picture. The reason the profile performs this core role is to enable user accounts to connect through social networks. Finding and relating to others would be a challenge without defining details. Also many network functions, such as comparisons between gaming scores, sharing, upvoting, liking, etc. would not be possible without user identification. Furthermore, to what is more important relating to this research, user identification is one of the main details in case of   online incidences, where it is written widely on further paragraphs. It is standard practice that social media sites do not provide access to the posts of registered users from non-registered users. Perhaps the only anomaly is Wikipedia, which allows users without getting an account to  access the free online encyclopedia as well as make simple edits. However, Wikipedia automatically detects and tracks user IP addresses for users who do not create accounts or log in to their profile prior to making edits, and logs these addresses for any edits produced.   However, even   though m ost of  platforms require  personal data due to registration and creation of a user-profile, it  may be useless as an attempt at identity verification. This, because it is easy to enter false data, also it is very easy to create an email address using false information , which it is used as a valid email address for the registration. None of the data is verified in any way and the sites are 'open to all' as a result, so users with pseudo identities are free to join and uninhibited to view/upload content. There is some kind of constraint with websites such as Facebook, where a person  must be ‘invited’ as a ‘friend’ in order to be able to interact with another user.   However, most of these sites make a user part of “community” upon registration without  a  identity verification. This lack of iden tity verification has led in s everal  legal issues for platforms.  For  example  “My Space”  was sued for negligence for the lack of age verification on its platform after a number of minor members  were sexually assaulted by other members of “MySpace”. Social networking providers enable the growth of online social networks by connecting a profile to those of other users and/or communities.   This feature of social media technology is one of the most contentious, since it may be argued that earlier networking technologies (such as telephony) enabled social networks to be created, encouraged and managed by individuals, implying that online communication technologies could not be recognized as uniquely social. This issue is exacerbated by the fact that "the nature and nomenclature of these connections may differ from site to site.”   One way to create an online social network, for example, is by creating a list of people you wish to communicate with, and then organizing   connections across that list. Facebook and VK name those "friends" on a list, "follow er s" on Twitter and Instagram, and "connections" on  Linkedin . If a list of network connections is created, by communicating with a list of those actively coordinated users, users can then check, access and change the social network. Consumption of content (such as accessing and sharing links) and interaction (Such  as “liking” or commenting) are also correlated with the creation of these lists, as a type of homepage that aggregates content from others in the list is typically given to users.   Global accessibility of Social Networks  Access  to  social networks goes beyond the geographic boarders, all what is needed is an internet connection. To take only one example, Facebook, founded in 2004, has gone from being a website used primarily by university students in North America to being used in over 70 languages by  2.8 billion monthly active users , at the last count.  Hence, all these online network connection mentioned beyond  has no limitation between users. For  example  a user in Europe can make an comment in a post of another user in USA and take a reply from a third user in Asia while he is talking with an online “friend” in South Africa.  This global accessibility of  u ser-generated content  raises question of jurisdiction and applicable law when attempting to address online illegal activity. For example , a n action that may lead one person to feel aggrieved in his or her jurisdiction may not be unlawful in the jurisdiction of the other party.   The Brussels Regulation sets out provision for determining jurisdiction in  the  European Union and a few other signatories, nevertheless, User-Generated Content originated from countries that are not a party to Brussels Regulation can face difficulties for the law.  An example of this is hate speech, where US law and European law differ on this issue, with the US leaning towards freedom of speech, while European Law is more restrictive (this issue is going to be wider treated on the second part of this chapter). Although in theory the access and operation of social media have  no  limit s  among countries with internet infrastructure, still these platforms become one of the most significant threats not only to authoritarian and quasi-authoritarian governments, but even to liberal democracies.  An initial step that regimes often take when trying to limit free expression and the threat of revolt is to close down not necessarily the entire internet, but social media in particular.  For example Turkey did this in 2014 when temporarily blocked  T witter after prime minister attacked this platform or when  In order to ban social media such as  Whatsapp  and other services, the Iranian prosecutor gave the government thirty days.   There are a variety of legal questions raised by these acts. In specific, are there situations when governments (such as during emergencies or riots) can legally ban social media platforms, or do people have an unlimited access to social media regardless of the circumstances?   If these services are determined to be a right, what are the grounds for acknowledging this? Furthermore, what has been the role of social media under authoritarian governments and where is it likely to lead? It is also clear that uncertainty exists even with the recognition of these commonalities; in fact because social media functionalities are incorporated into items that are not typically developed for social networking purposes. However, this collection of commonalities helps to start addressing an emerging set of connections unique to a number of interesting Internet-based apps that are gradually building connections between users and virtual space content created by users. And with the development of these connections, certain legal issues emerge that will be further dealt with. The creation and sharing of information or media created by users as a medium contributes to the integration of technology, telecommunications, and social networking. It is content that developers openly build on a publicly open platform.  While this encourages freedom of expression, by giving rise to incidences of hate speech,  rumour  mongering, data fraud or hacking, to name a few, it can also serve as a double-edged sword.  Thus, Andreas Kaplan and Michael  Haenlein  characterize social media as "a group of internet-based apps that are built on Web 2.0's ideological and technological foundations and allow user-generated content to be created and exchanged.” As it was mentioned before,  creating  a profile in one of the social media forms  does not  take much time and effort. All that is required is a working email address to which a confirmation email is sent automatically upon registration, or sometimes even only a phone number or just the existence of profile of another social network. Once confirmed the profile comes to existence. There is no real mechanism to verify the veracity of the registrant’s personal details. As such, names of popular personalities can be appropriated to create false profiles leading to serious ramifications such as commercial exploitation, defamation, ridicule and blackmail.  When Facebook first announced that users could customize URLs (Uniform Resource Locators) identifying the locations of their profile pages to include their user-names, Larry  Winget , the author of several finance books, was preempted by a fan who chose a face-to- face meeting if the link, 'facebook.com/ larrywinget ' was to be turned over to  Winget .  Spurious profiles can also be used to encourage naive users to share information that can be used for phishing attack.  For example, by pretending to be as a person’s real world friend (by opening a fake profile), it is possible to trick someone into giving personal important information. Creation of fake profiles of celebrities can be used also for undesired purposes. When a user creates his / her profile in the social network, communicating with that user serves as a gateway for others linked to the social network. A profile in  social networking is similar to a user's online identity, or digital personality. The profile is a repository of data, which are more often than not personal to the user. Though, the users have an option not to provide certain personal data, they are tempted to do so, given the provocative nature of social networking. So, massive flows of personal  informations  are attracted by social networks, which are vulnerable to severe privacy and identity threats. As user profiles contain personal information, some of which may be sensitive, such as addresses to date of birth, unjustified access to such details constitutes a serious threat. Unless users set their privacy settings carefully, such information can be publicly accessible resulting in embarrassment or damage to repute.   There are cases of lost employment opportunities , blackmail  and defamation  that resulted out of the use of information posted on social networks profiles. Further, secondary data such as likes and dislikes of users are  preciouse  for targeted advertising and spam. The option of some social medias to upload photographs in profile raise level of the risk. Many of these sites allow users to tag  images with metadata, such as links to other profiles or email addresses resulting in greater possibilities for unwanted linkage to personal information. Even if users are vigilant about the photos they share with, their privacy can be at greater risk from images posted by others. Very few social networks offer privacy tools to control the tagging of images with links to their profile or the accessibility of tagged images in search results. The above-mentioned discussion indicates that creating a "safe virtuality relationship" will be very difficult without adequate security of online privacy.   1.2.   A comparative overview of the relevant legal frameworks relating to social media platforms The prospect of state regulation of social media has been proposed as a solution to issues such as fake news, hate speech, conspiracy-mongering, and related ills in recent discussions over the internet's effect on global democracy. Regulations have been implemented in Europe in the form of regulations such as the Network Enforcement Law ( NetzDG )  in Germany, in EU  parliament also has start discussing and working in Digital Service Act   (DSA) and Digital Markets Acts   (DMA ) .  Below is a comparative overview of relevant national and regional legal framework and approaches   to regulating social networks. Germany Germany in 2015 as a result of its government decision to accept  im migrants coming mostly from Syria (that was suffering from the war) faced an increase of online hate speech. Many of the far right, the neo-nazi right, anti immigrants right strongly started to use facebook, youtube and twitter in order to insight hatred against immigrants who were coming in Germany. The minister of justice of Germany concerned from the situation wrote to Facebook complaining on their non-compliance with their own policy in order to deal with the major problem that is a public policy problem (because of content there were inciting violence in streets, fire bombings of a refugee center ). Trying to fix this situation, the government invited the main companies and they created a non-binding code of conduct that the companies were map to follow. In this non-binding code of conduct the companies had agreed essentially to take down content that was concepted as hate speech under German law. Over the time the results were not satisfactory, this soft law was not effecting in the companies compliance with their anti-hate speech policy, hence the government moved towards on creating a hard law known as the Network Enforcement Act (NetsDG). At the end of 2017 this act came in effect as the first law to regulate social networks.  However, it does not target specifically  the  diversity of opinion, but rather at securing a free speech. It obliges platforms to speedily delete illegal content such as hate speech. This law applies to companies with more than two million registered users in the country.  They were required to develop procedures to review complaints about content they were hosting, delete anything that was apparently illegal within 24 hours, and publish updates on how they were doing every six months.  Individuals may be fined up to EUR 5 m illion  and platforms up to EUR 50 m illion  for not fulfilling these requirements.  The first fine under this law was issued to Facebook in July 2019 where the company had to pay EUR 2 m illion  for under-reporting illegal activity on its platforms in Germany.  The company complained that the new law had lacked clarity. EU The German hard  s anction oriented model is a model that has been seen as very attractive around the world. The Nets DG model is basically spreading its traveling around the world, it has been somewhat resisted in Brussels so the European commission has been mainly focused on the non-binding approach similar to the very first part of the German model but that is moving. There was a tremendous amount of pressure on the European Union to do something about it after the parliamentary elections.  EU  P arliament has start ed  working in Digital Service Act   (DSA) and Digital Markets Acts   (DMA). The DSA and the DMA have two key goals: to create a safer digital space in which the fundamental rights of all users of digital services are  protected and to develop a fair playing field to increase innovation, development, and competitiveness, both in the European Single Market and globally. On the EU parliament debate of 10 th  of February 2021 MEPs called on the EU to step up its efforts to regulate social media while safeguarding freedom of speech and avoiding censorship.  These acts will provide rules for platforms as well as solutions for tackling harmful or illegal content online, such as disinformation.  MEPs praised these attempts to regulate the online world through laws, not platform guidelines, but said they must preserve freedom of speech and fundamental rights, while preventing censorship. European Commission Vice President Věra Jourová said that the proposed Digital Services Act aims to increase the accountability of online platforms and clarify the rules about taking down illegal content, including hate speech and incitement to violence: "We need to bring order to the digital expression of democracy and to end the digital Wild West.” She committed to proposing rules for online political advertising. Other speakers were more concerned about the danger to freedom of expression. Geert Bourgeois (ECR, Belgium) warned that the notice and action system will lead to censorship. “Platforms will have to run every notification through their algorithm and the consequence will be overly politically correct censorship,” he said. “Freedom of expression must be our starting point" and restrictions on freedom of speech an exception. “There are countries where censorship is banned in the constitution  - let it also be the case for the EU,” he said. UK In 15 December of 2020 Digital Secretary of UK Oliver Dowden and Home Secretary Priti Patel announced the government’s final decisions on new laws regarding to regulation of  s ocial  m edia  c ompanies  (‘ Online Safety Bill ’) .   This regulations will apply to any company in the world hosting user-generated content online accessed by people in the UK or allowing them to privately or openly communicate with others online. It includes social media, platforms for video sharing and instant messaging, online forums, dating apps, websites for commercial pornography, as well as online markets, peer-to-peer services, websites for consumer cloud storage and video games that enable online interaction. The new regulations will also apply to search engines. Online journalism will be exempted, as will reader comments on such sites, from news publishers' websites. The legislation will include specific measures to ensure that journalistic content is still protected when it is re-shared on social media platforms. At the core of the measures is safety of  people, in particular safety of  children. In order to remove and reduce the dissemination of illegal content such as child pornography abuse, terrorist material, and suicide content, social networking platforms, websites, apps and other resources that host user-generated content or encourage users to talk to others online will need to be removed and limited. The Government is also making steps in consulting with the Law Commission on whether it can make the promotion of self-harm illegal. In order to protect children from exposure to harmful content or activities such as grooming, bullying and pornography, social network platforms will have to do much more. The legislation will provide safeguards for online freedom of speech and pluralism - ensuring the rights of people to share in and engage in robust discussion in society.  In a strategy based on sites, apps and platforms where the possibility of harm is highest, platforms will have varying liabilities for specific types of content and operation. The most popular social network platforms with the largest audiences and high-risk features (likely to include Facebook, Twitter, Instagram will be in category 1) will have to go further by establishing and enforcing clear terms and conditions that explicitly state how content that is legal but could cause significant physical or psychological harm to adults will be handled.   Office of Communications (Ofcom) is going to be the regulator body with the power to fine companies failing in their duty of care up to 18 m British pounds or ten percent of annual global turnover, whichever is higher. This regulator body will have also the power to block non-compliant services from being accessed in the UK. The legislation contains provisions to enforce criminal penalties on senior managers. These powers may take place in situations where companies do not take the new rules seriously - for example, if they do not respond thoroughly, correctly and in a timely manner to Ofcom's requests for information. The power will be introduced by Parliament by secondary legislation, and the reservation of compliance power follows analogous approaches in other sectors, such as the regulation of financial services.  US In 2020 , Section 230 of the Communications Decency Act of 1996 came under attack from members of both political parties.   Section 230 of Communications Decency Act is an federal law which gives to internet companies protection from liability for user-generated content disseminated on   their   platforms . U.S. government is right deciding what precisely ought to happen to Section 230 of the Communications Decency Act. Later events as well as continuous   antitrust   concerns   suggest   that   early   within   the   Biden-Harris   administration,   they   will   anticipate   a   vigorous   examination   of   how   the  direction may well be adjusted to superior protect the public from harmful content. At the moment, no less than 23 bills that would amend Section 230 have been presented in Congress.  The proposed amendments fall in three main categories: The first approach would decrease the scope of the protection offered to platforms by the law or require platforms to alter their behavior to keep those protections. By exposing the companies to more litigation, the considering goes, encouraging them to protect users from possibly hurtful or discriminatory content. The challenge here is to do so whereas striking a balance that does not energize  overmoderation  of marginalized communities.   The second one, which incorporates the Online Freedom and Viewpoint Diversity Act, would limit balance and fact checking to promote a more liberated flow of ideas. The third group of proposals, would basically destroy Section 230.  These proposition  appear to be created to urge Big company’s consideration more than to really advocate a return to an advanced Wild West. But they too highlight the way Section 230, in spite of its flaws, makes a difference to bring a few arrange to the online world. Russia n Federation Human Rights Watch reported that Russian authorities lately are escalating pressure on Social media platforms, forcing them to censor online content consider illegal by the government.  They have been facing fines and potential blocking in failure to comply with Russian’s internet legislation. A new law came into effect on 10 January 2021, imposing fines of up to 10% of every company's annual revenue for websites that do not censor "illegal"  content( Federal Law of 30.12.2020 N 511-FZ "On Amendments to the Code of the Russian Federation on Administrative Offenses" ) Also on 1 st  of February another law came into effect, under which large social media networks are obliged to take down content deemed illegal under the Russian Law( Federal Law of December 30, 2020 No. 530-FZ "On Amendments to the Federal Law" On Information, Information Technologies and Information Protection" ). On 4 th  of February, the upper chamber of the Russian Parliament called for legislative amendments enforcing fines on social networking sites, as well as temporary and total blocking of entire platforms, to disseminate calls for participation in demonstrations.  This also influenced from the protests organized through social media. The foreign companies are facing pressure from the authorities to stop “restricting the freedom of speech” in relation with state media content published in social media. Hence, A new bill was signed on 30 December, empowering the authorities to block websites censoring Russian state media content.   In January, the upper chamber of the Russian Parliament began work on a new bill proposing fines for social media companies that "illegally block users." The proposal came in response to Twitter and Facebook's moves to block the accounts of former US President Donald Trump and aims to prevent the potential blocking of Russian politicians' social media profiles. China China does not promote self-regulation, but promotes self-discipline. Example may be the launch of net.china.cn as a reporting center for improper and illegal content.  China has initiated a set of new laws and regulations extending the legal framework for its control over the Internet, thus centralizing authority over social media in the hands of high-level decision-makers.   Beijing has passed new laws on national security and cyber security, establishing a legislative framework for mechanisms of free speech control that have long been practiced by the police and the courts but have not been codified . As the result of Web 2.0 sites creation the Chinese government in 2004 passed the public pledge on self-discipline for the Chinese Internet Industry and the 2004 provisional provisions for administering the development of public information services on instant messaging tool which placed more priority on regulation of platform than on content regulation. The Golden Shield Project often referred as the Great Firewall of China (2006-2008) was implemented by the government as a censorship and surveillance project which it can access records of any citizen and delete them for being harmful.  There are an average of 30,000 government censors in place that delete anti-government posts or block foreign sites seen as unfavorable to Chinese internet users. State Council order N.292, provides that websites which are based in China cannot link to other social media websites from around the globe. Moreover, this order creates liability on providers for content verification (Art.11) also gives the  government the authority to assess any record (Art.14).  Legislations such as Rules for Interim Regulations of the People’s Republic of China on the Management of International Computer Information Networking and Regulations for Administering the Internet News and Information Service regulate the online activities monitoring.  This monitoring process is undergone by government departments such as Ministry of Public Security, Ministry of Information Industry, Public Information Network Security Supervision Bureau, Information Steering group of the State Council, China National Information Security Testing Evaluation and Certification Centre (CNISTEC). Tort Liability Law, 2010 enacted by China makes a network service provider jointly and severally liable as a party infringing the civil interest or rights of another party. Civil interests are a broad category that includes the right to privacy on the Internet, copyright etc. Government agencies either manually remove illegal content through censors or temporarily auto-block them on the basis of keyword filters and then judge their nature. China has an Internet police force of 2 million to monitor people's actions.  China also announced in Science Military Strategy 2015 that cyber warfare is its top military priority. Chapter 2. Conflicting rights in the operation of social media platforms 2. 1 . Content moderation on social media Content  m oderation  on  s ocial  m edia  is the practice through which a platform that deals with user-generated speech makes decisions about whether to host or continue hosting a specific content, or to grant the content relative prominence or prioritization, under the “terms of service” rules. P latforms  having these  rules in place  aim to  prevent users from sharing such types of  content , such as abuse, child sexual assault, and hate  speech  or expression.  An operator can temporarily or permanently ban users who violate its policies , depending on the seriousness of the violation in the operator's judgment. Since there is no standardized framework for content moderation, policies differ across social media platforms. Some operators have opted to issue data about their content moderation activities, such as the volume of content deleted and the number of appeals, but in general operators are not obliged to do so  either by law or be their own rules .  Lately there is a lot of interest globally in content moderation. Where governments ask platforms to take proactive steps to prohibit such forms of material from being circulated online, public policy experts and scholars question the level to which such efforts endanger free speech.  When a company dominates a big part of the social media market, as Facebook and Twitter do, it becomes not only an economic gatekeeper, but also a human rights gatekeeper – particularly in terms of expression and privacy.   There are many forms of content that authorities want to see removed.   This include illegal material such as racist expressions, discriminatory speech based on religion, ethnicity, or sexual orientation, and statements denying crimes against  humanity such as the Holocaust.   Another form of content that authorities are countering is that which promotes and also acts as propaganda for terrorist organizations; there is also the issue of pornography and sex on public social networks, as well as for young people ; t here is also the issue of live streaming of violent videos, such as the Christchurch  mosque attack in France on 15 march 2019;  Finally, there is disinformation and intentionally false information fabricated to destabilize election cycles both within and outside the countries involved in the elections. Decisions on the importance of content decide how many and which groups of users are subjected to it, and these decisions are taken using multiple standards and processes. A decision could mean extending the scope and visibility of those forms of speech, or it could mean reducing or restricting the exposure.   To classify content to mark or delete, social media  platforms  mostly  depend on three sources: (1)  content moderators , (2)  users , and (3) automated systems, also known as artificial intelligence (AI) technologies.   Users may flag or mark offensive posts for inspection and removal by content moderators. Posts can also be flagged and removed by automated systems  (artificial intelligence) . Content moderators, who are mainly contractors, may be able to detect subtle abuses of content policies, such as taking the context of a statement into account. The content which platforms often ban and remove include both content that is illegal and content that,  irrespective of  its legality, the internet service considers undesirable.  For example, conversations about sex or certain kind of discriminatory hateful speech that can be legal but however because of its negative effects they may be considered from platforms as undesirable content. What constitutes legal internet content varies by country and territory. I n the case where  a qualified legal body  order s  a  platform  to delete content, the  platform  is  not exercising content moderation decision. It is subject to a compulsory takedown.   In comparison,  a company  can decide to delete illegal content without receiving a request from authorities, based solely on its terms of service. Taking steps to uphold the terms of service, which may contain a ban on legal or illegal content, is voluntary.   Hence , the only time platforms  are  forced to remove content is when  it is  ordered to do so by an independent and impartial judicial body. Furthermore, in order to uphold human rights, laws affecting speech must adhere to the principles of legality, necessity, and proportionality, have a valid purpose, and ensure that those affected obtain due process.   Content  m oderation in  s ocial media in contrary is a process undertaken from the companies to apply their rules and which is different from a legal process described.  Despite the fact that  companies  are not technically obliged to include due process while engaging in content moderation, they have a responsibility to protect human rights while developing and implementing their terms of service policies under the international human rights framework.  Platforms  must also provide access to remedy whether their content moderation induces, leads to, or is related to human rights violations.   According to the UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights, companies must not only comply with legal inquiries and judicial procedures, but also go beyond and above what the courts demand by engaging in non-judicial grievance processes that represent groups whose rights may have been breached.   Governments have the duty, within the international human rights framework, to prevent, prosecute, sanction, and seek remedies for human rights violations by “effective policies, legislation, regulations, and adjudication.”   In the case of content moderation, this means the requirement to establish legal guarantees based on the human right to freedom of speech, which requires access to information. A group of actors are concerned that self-regulation may not be enough to solve these problems.   Some civil society groups, for example, campaign for the implementation of human rights-based standards for content moderation into legislation   in order to preserve freedom of speech beyond the will of  platforms.    Such a project should be carefully examined and carried out with caution.     Some of the alleged solutions to content moderation issues proposed by governments and other internet players endanger human rights, such as proposals to explicitly ban certain types of speech or to hold companies accountable for filtering all content on their  platforms  to avoid potential damage. Content moderation decisions have an impact on users' ability to express themselves and receive information online.   There is an effect on both an individual and a group level. This is because individual takedown actions have a collective effect, influencing the discussion space and potentially silencing the voices of whole communities. This poses a significant danger, especially for vulnerable or marginalized groups. As a result,  it  is recommended that  when  platforms  establish rules for content moderation, they must ensure that the  rules  are lawful,   correspond to international human rights principles, and are freely accepted by users. Another issue regarding content moderation is the fact that it is not completely clear how these companies make content moderation decisions.  According to recent research, content takedowns are essentially discretionary and subject to the will of the companies . This is especially concerning in a situation where authorities are putting public pressure on companies to conduct content moderation at rising speed and accuracy, far beyond real-world technological capability.   Inconsistency is unavoidable , since the human beings that make these decisions work under tight deadlines with little training and in the cases when the  moderation content is done by Artificial Intelligence, the latter can fail to understand the context of language used.  Moreover,  users often have little   opportunity to react to content  removed , claim the authenticity of the content, or  get remedy  for unlawful removal.   The United Nations Special Rapporteur on the Promotion and Protection of the Right of Freedom Of  opinion  and expression has called for more transparency and accountability in content moderation decisions.   As a result, some  platforms , including leading players like Facebook, have started to reveal more details about their internal processes and are looking for ways to be more transparent about moderation decision-making. The non-profit organization “Access now” ( that protects and expands the digital rights of people around the world ) provides in their preliminary recommendations basic human rights principles for content moderation, in order to prevent, address, or mitigate human rights violations and to promote enjoyment of human rights.  According  to  their recommendation any content moderation processes designed and deployed by platforms, should adhere to principles such as Prevention of harm; Evaluation of impact; Transparency; Proportionality; Context; Non-discrimination; Human decision; Notice; Remedy. 2. 2 . The changing nature of the concept of privacy on social media Privacy throughout history  has evolved significantly.   What is considered private, however, varies depending on the  era , culture, and  the individual .   The transition from the conventional view of privacy to online privacy shows that the  notion  of 'privacy' must be rethought.     The concept of privacy begins to emerge at the end of the nineteenth century, most notably in the legal scholarly literature in the United States  where  W arren  & B randies   (1890) describe privacy as "the right to be left alone" in their seminal paper.  Soon after, a variety of international and national bodies incorporated this right into their legal texts and regulations.   Numerous international human rights conventions, both universal and regional, include provisions concerning the right to privacy.  For example article 12 of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (United Nations, 1948), International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights on article 17 include the similar provision   (United Nations 1966), article 8 of the European Convention of Human Rights (Council of Europe, 1950), also article 7 of the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union   specify that the right to privacy is a fundamental human right and that everyone has the right to have his or her private and family life, home, and correspondence respected, and also the right to be protected from any unlawful interference.   These  provisions , however, are very brief and do not have detailed instructions about what privacy is or what forms of privacy may be legally covered.  A significant contribution for these questions has the case law, the decisions of the courts such as  the  European Court of Human Rights (ECtHR) and the European Union’s European Court of Justice (CJEU) that guarantee the application of these conventions.  However, legal protection for a citizen from  violation  of his or her privacy is just a bare minimum that must be met. Other norms and rules can also apply. Bodies in various sectors, for example, can implement separate and more strict self-regulation and co-regulation guidelines. The ever-changing information and communication technologies make a normative approach to personal privacy far more complicated.  In an offline situation, observable objects and marks, for instance, usually mark the borders between private and public domains, and therefore the size of private space are often determined in units of distance. Although varying opinions exist about how small or big an individual's private space  ought to be , the distinction between where public space  ends and personal space begins are often easily observed and settled upon during a given community.  It  can no t  be said an equivalent for the virtual online world where the concept of space may be a metaphor. In such an area  there  i s  no unit of measurement and there are not any markers to  div ide   the private and public sphere. To confuse matters more, people from diverse backgrounds, often with radically different privacy values and standards, co-occupy this abstract and metaphorical space. In such an online world, normative rules and standards for privacy are  thought to be  irrelevant.   As a result, the responsibility of protecting personal privacy shifts to individuals. Not only   the traditional mode of communication  has  been totally transformed in the Digital age – people  also  can share the most personal moments of their (or others') lives without almost any limitations  through social media platforms  –   and  monitoring by private organizations is now very common and severe  as well .   People expose themselves explicitly by making information of their private lives publicly accessible on various social media platforms .  When more people  will ingly post personal information about themselves and others online, the 'digital footprint' of these individuals has grown steadily over the past few years.   As a consequence, a substantial percentage of privacy issues and monitoring are caused not just by the state or its institutions, but also by  companies , other users, and – the most recent component of the problem – by the individual himself/herself. Concerns over privacy when sharing information in a public or semi-public space are intensified by the functional features of  social media platforms , which can obscure the true audience of these disclosures due to technical properties (e.g., searchability) and usage dynamics (e.g., invisible audiences). Privacy on  social media  is a multifaceted issue that requires user  to  focus  on   protect ing  privacy from third-party data collection  while  maintain ing  personal impressions through a variety of contexts and relationships. David Flaherty draw s  attention to the responsibility of the individual in this technologically  advanced  world, where there may are many devices which might pose a threat to privacy.  He says “ you have to be your own privacy commissioner. And you have to decide, in your own life, to the extent that you can do it, where you want to draw the line between openness and  candour ; or, to what extent you want to control your personal privacy. You reflect on it: all of us protect our personal privacy day in and day out by various strategies that we have developed. ”   Taking into consideration the upper citation and the technology itself, the education of users about technology, more specifically about how  social media platforms  work regarding privacy is a key issue. In many cases  a  violation of online privacy is caused by the  user’s  lack of knowledge regarding the privacy settings provided by platforms.  Educating users about their online footprints seems to be a more promising goal than reforming legislation or admonish researchers and other audiences to act differently  in relation to online  content .   Despite norms and customs influencing “privacy issues offline, of which a series of well-established cultural, social, and legal norms may be applied,” the responsibility of online privacy security is carried mainly by an individual's own conscientious effort.   According to  Bernhard   Debatin , for online social media users, “their level of privacy safety is proportional to the number of friends  (followers) , their criteria for accepting friends, and the quantity and nature of personal data provided” online.   These risks can be  minimized in certain ways by restricting friending habits, privacy settings, and disclosures. 2.3 . Freedom of expression vs.  the  right to privacy The connection between the right to privacy and the right to freedom of expression is complex. Both human rights are inalienable and are usually mutually supportive and interdependent.  They have a vital role along with the values of autonomy, identity and dignity in the  realisation  of human self-development helping to reach full potential of individuals.  Now-days, almost every act online is an act of expression. The right to privacy is frequently considered a fundamental requirement for the  realisation  of the right to freedom of expression,  insofar as privacy protection plays an imperative part in creation of the content necessary for the proper exercise of the right to freedom of speech and opinion.  In brief, privacy assurance helps with creating the content for unhindered freedom of expression. The protection of private spaces against other ’ s interference be they physical spaces such as home and other private places, or virtual spaces like social network groups encourages people to communicate more openly with each other, thus, privacy protection upgrades mutual trust by securing confidentiality among selected people, and thus empowers free flow of information and freedom of expression and opinions among these people. The other side of the coin is that freedom of expression equally enhances the right to privacy online as well as offline, in that level that freedom of expression and its correlative right to freedom of information are basic to the protection of privacy. As interdependent rights, freedom of expression and privacy have an intersection regarding the notion of reputation. Defamation law provides protection for person's reputation and dignity, which can be related to the privacy of the person. On the other hand, if the law on defamation extended too much on protection for an individual's dignity, honor and privacy, it might restrict the right of others to freedom of expression, particularly in cases where there is no provision for truth and  defence  of public interest.  Freedom to hold opinions may also be threatened by the law of defamation if the defense of fair comments is not valid.  Satire and caricature are both included in freedom of speech, and this may in some cases override. In other words, as to whether or not a specific instance of defamatory expression is protected, there could be either illegitimate or legitimate restrictions to either privacy or freedom of expression. It is essential also for human political life to have interdependence and mutual support between the right to privacy and the right to freedom of expression. These rights uphold the right to vote, conduct political meetings and free associations away from the public's glare and without fear of reprisals.  For this purpose, sufficient privacy protection, as well as freedom of speech, promotes more active engagement in politics. How to balance the privacy rights of public figures and individuals engaged in public life with the defense of the freedom of speech of others is often an ongoing discussion in all jurisdictions in the practice of law. The information element of privacy is about individuals controlling and disseminating personal information within those self-selected boundaries. This could conflict with the fact that the right to freedom of expression guarantees the rights of individuals to possess, try, obtain and impart any kind of information and ideas. It also concerns the interest and personal need of data subjects to track information  themselves or via approved parties and people's ability to access and disseminate information.  From a legal viewpoint there are mutual exceptions or derogations in most jurisdictions. For example, Article 10 of the ECHR, in paragraph 2, stipulates that the exercise of the right to freedom of expression must be subject to "the protection of the reputation   or rights of others, for preventing the disclosure of information received in confidence, or for maintaining the authority and impartiality of the judiciary”.  Then Article 8 of the ECHR, makes provision  in  Paragraph  2  that:  “ There shall be no interference by a public authority with the exercise of this right, except such as is in accordance with the law and is necessary in a democratic society, [...] or for the protection of the rights and freedoms of others”.  According to a 2016 publication of UNESCO with topic “Privacy, free expression and transparency ” where it is mentioned that  “In legal practice, the mutual restriction between the right to privacy and the right to freedom of expression can be  organised  in four general types of situations, under which the right to privacy of an individual conflicts with the right to freedom of speech of other individuals or legal persons”. In the first situation, privacy of an individual can be violated, even if the exercise of freedom of expression and other related rights does not directly affect the person.  This can happen where the simple distribution of information in an online context can lead to an invasion of the individual's solitude or seclusion (and therefore the private sphere).  In this context, the specific manner in which someone expresses himself / herself and distributes information, even if it relates solely to him / herself, can still constitute an invasion of the privacy of another, in the sense of violating the meaning of privacy of “leave me alone”.  For example, the Facebook account/profile of a user may be flooded with messages and updates from someone, which messages the user would not be interested in at all, but within a context where the user does not have an option to unsubscribe or disconnect for some reason or another.  Too much  self-expression in social networking sites, while ignoring the expectations of others for mental solitude and integrity in a "private virtual sphere," can compromise their expectations of privacy and infringe the civility rules that exist in personal relationships. The second situation corresponds to SPAM (i.e. unsolicited) received e-mails, messages, leaflets and similar materials against the receiver's  will,  e.g.  In the absence of a receiver's subscription even if these communications were explicitly and truthfully presented as commercial in nature.  It is because while such messages will in a way operationalize the senders 'right to freedom of expression, they also represent intrusions into the recipient's private domain.  The third situation involves the exercise of the right to freedom of assembly, which, if not properly exercised, may have an effect on the right of others to privacy in both online and offline contexts. While the right to freedom of assembly is guaranteed by many national laws and permitted in a manner specified by law, even a law-allowed assembly can  jeopardise  the expectations of private and ordinary daily life that residents living adjacent to an approved public meeting place may have. Online protests or assemblies may take different forms targeting private Internet domains, such as widespread DDoS attacks; coordinated spamming in comment forums to protest online content; and coordinated spamming emails against other individuals. The fourth situation refers to potential conflicts between an individual, X's, the right to privacy and another individual, Y's, freedom of expression where Y's statements affect X in different ways. The case here can be when Y spread his personal story due to a  aoutobiography  where he/she disclose private information from his/her private life and in these  informations  is included X also without him/her  consent, in this way potentially breaching X’s right to privacy.   A practical example it is the case of a public celebrity who wrote about her sexual life in details without the permission of her ex-husband. Her Freedom of speech to disseminate information about her personal life conflicts with the right to privacy of her ex-husband. The invasion of the other’s privacy online in context of self-expression happens also in publication of photographs. When the shared photograph/s contain images of individual/s from whom is not taken a permission, thus, threatening their (or some of them) right to privacy. These kind of cases are on increase due to the fast development of the technology regarding with the smartphones and other similar devices with high definition cameras, which enables capturing moments of our life in every moment and to publish them very fast in time and digital space.   Balancing the right to privacy on the one side with the right to freedom of expression on the other, has been a key concern of media lawyers. Of such freedoms, the principle of international human rights law is that restrictions of rights must be justified by reference to pre-existing laws that permit proportionate action necessary to achieve a valid goal such as national security, public order, or the rights of others.  At the same time, human rights claim to protect freedom of expression and the right to privacy but how do you balance and put these rights into practice? In general, there are two balancing categories: balancing by definition and ad hoc balancing. Balancing by definition. The first method is to identify the precise scopes of each right by creating specific rules to avoid future conflict; for example, by specifically prescribing which kinds of speeches are not allowed because they breach the privacy of another without following international standards such as the necessity test. An example may be the confidentiality duty of individuals who work in law  firms or medical service and must not disclose personal information of their clients to a third party.    Limiting freedom of expression in the interests of privacy would therefore need to be justified by the criteria of necessity, and thus the concept of proportionality. Both rights can however be limited by other  imperatives .                     Many materials that are prohibited in different ways are hate speeches that amount to promoting incitement to violence, aggression or prejudice, including those related to gender, ethnicity, sexual orientation and religion.  The derogations or restrictions imposed by law include extreme situations of international agreement such as the spread of child pornography materials. As the UN Human Rights Council has reported, it is a common norm of national legislation to ban offensive speeches like online child pornography that violates the dignity and privacy of children. A major advantage for this method of balancing is that this would usually occur through legislation that will open up the topic for sufficient public debate and review before taking a decision on the possible exemptions from scope, process, derogations,  and public interest. Ad hoc balancing. Much practice balancing nowadays is done by capable courts, for example, regional courts, supreme courts, constitutional courts or national courts in an ad hoc manner. Conflicts are resolved in this context by addressing new situations before adjudicating courts with the intention of  redelining  or redefining the legal limits of privacy and freedom of expression by gradually improving case law in this area. While there are differences in the balancing mechanisms used in the different jurisdictions and in the gravity they give to the two freedoms, these balancing mechanisms or processes generally meet the test well dealt with in General Comment 27 of the Human Rights Committee. Accordingly, in order to determine whether a restriction can be justified, The limit should meet requirements such as  those defined in the Special Rapporteur on the Promotion and Protection of the Right to Freedom of Opinion and Expression: “a. Any restrictions must be provided by the law; b. The essence of a human right is not subject to restrictions; c. Restrictions must be necessary in a democratic society; d. Any discretion exercised when implementing the restrictions must not be unfettered; e. For a restriction to be permissible, it is not enough that it serves one of the enumerated legitimate aims. It must be necessary for achieving the legitimate aim; f. Restrictive measures must conform to the principle of proportionality, they must be appropriate to achieve their protective function, they must be the least intrusive instrument amongst those which might achieve the desired result, and they must be proportionate to the interest to be protected”. In  Einarsson  v. Iceland   The European Court of Human Rights has found that Icelandic courts  failed to protect  the right to private life of a famous media figure by refuting his allegation of defamation against an internet user who called him a “rapist”.  The case involved a  well known  blogger, writer and media personality who was accused of rape by a woman. A week after the rape lawsuit was filed against him, an Instagram user posted a modified image of the media personality entitled “fu*k you rapper bastard”. National courts refused to consider the Instagram user responsible for the defamation because his comment was a judgment of value in context. The European Court of Human Rights disagreed with the national courts 'decision, stating that national courts had failed to strike a proper balance between the media personality right to protect his private life and the user's right to freedom of expression to Instagram. The European Court of Human Rights took special issues with the classification of the term "rapist" as a judgment of values by local courts, rather than a statement of fact. As a result, under Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights, she found a violation of the media's right to privacy for his private life.  By considering the following requirements, the Court evaluated whether the domestic courts had balanced the two freedoms at stake: a. How well known was Mr.  Einarsson , the subject matter and Mr.  Einarsson’s  conduct prior to the publication of the impugned statement; b. Contribution to a debate of general interest; c. Content, form and consequences of the impugned publication. In the context of online platforms, the European Court on Human Rights has emphasized the importance of freedom of expression in the European Convention on Human Rights. In the case  Tamiz  v. The United Kingdom   t he  ECHR found that the United Kingdom had not failed in its duty to protect the right to privacy and family life of a person who was the target of alleged defamatory comments made anonymously in the comment section of a blog hosted by Google's "Blogger .com "service. The U.K. courts had dismissed the individual ’ s libel claim against Google Inc. The Court held that the domestic courts had properly balanced the laws of both information society service providers (ISSPs), such as Google Inc., and end-users of Blogger.com against the right of individuals to respect reputation, included in the right to a private and family life, under Article 8 by applying the  "real and substantial tort"  test when determining whether to allow service out of Google Inc.'s jurisdiction The ECtHR found the following considerations in deciding whether the UK had met its obligation to protect  Tamiz's  right to reputation.  Gravity of interference with private life , by applying the  "real and substantial tort"  the ECHR determined that the comments were not critical enough to threaten  Tamiz's  opportunity to exercise his right to reputational respect. They were too minor in nature and/or scope of dissemination to do serious harm to a person's reputation. The ECtHR stressed that posts of this type are popular on the Internet and are “little more than ‘vulgar abuse,'” and  Tamiz  as a public figure ( politician )  would be expected to tolerate them. The existence of a consensus among the Council of Europe's Contracting States on the value of the interests at stake.  The ECHR valued the important role of Google Inc. as an information society service provider which differ from a content producer. The court in support of this distinction referred to the  the Declaration on freedom of communication on the Internet (adopted in 2003 by the 840th meeting of Ministers’ Deputies, the Committee of Ministers of the Council of Europe) and Directive 2000/31/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council and other international declarations.  This international material stated that ISSPs could not be held liable for content that they did not create unless, after being informed and aware of illegal content, it does not take measures to remove the content. The ECtHR emphasized the importance of ensuring free dissemination of information, opinions, and debate, as well as the important function of ISSPs in this flow. The ECtHR concluded that, while the right to freedom of expression and the right to privacy are both essential, restricting freedom of expression in this case was not justified. 2. 4 . Personal data collection on social media The process of collecting, measuring, and analyzing accurate insights for research using standard validated techniques is known as data collection.  General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), considered as the world’s strongest set of data protection rules provides among others that data collection is a form of data processing under EU law.  Moreover, this right to protection of personal data it must be considered in relation to its function in society and be balanced against other fundamental rights (as it is the right of privacy or freedom of expression) in accordance with the principle of proportionality.   Personal data is  collected  from a number of sources using multiple datasets  combining  techniques.  Some of these personal data are given intentionally from  individuals, such as when filling out an online form. However, The majority of data is registered automatically, which is referred to as “digital breadcrumbs” that are collected unintentionally as a result of people’s online and offline activities. Social media platforms such as Facebook, Twitter and other big companies have the ability to collect information about their users. This data is collected through devices, while practicing freedom of expression on social media like posting, sharing, liking contents, accept, searching and many other online activity on platforms.  Big data companies and scientists gather this data and create personas  for users  that can define  their  age, gender, interests, and much more.  In principle  Big  data  companies ,   are developing computer-generated profiles based on  data  collected from  devices , which  end  up  generating analytical information or to predict future behaviors or developments.  It is estimated that the largest social media provider used over 52   000 personal attributes to categorize people's interests and characteristics.   According to some research, data mining techniques can predict a person's personality more effectively than any of their friends and relatives.  In a blog of Loyola University Maryland “3 ways that social media knows you better than your friends and family do” Ciara Wake writes three ways social media invades users privacy to stay a step ahead of them, their family and friends.   One of the ways she describes is the ability that platforms have to keep up with users friendships.  By monitoring postings, relationships and activities updates, they can predict their close friends. By the access they have on users’ phone and emails contacts they have the capability to scan these contacts and suggesting similar other users on social media.  The other one is their ability to track users online behavior and browsing history. Because of this, platforms are capable to generate a general idea on users interest, in this way the ads and some content shown on users social media account correspond this general idea generated from platforms.  The third one is the access the platforms have regarding users location. By using users devices location data, v arious platforms can advertise cafes, shops, and even friends in  users  area. In any case, the effect of profiling on a person's life is not negligible, since the information produced is then used to make decisions (automated or not) about a person or a group of people.   Decisions focused on profiling personalize an individual's informational environment to a high degree, a phenomenon known as microtargeting.   This microtargeting practices may have little impact on certain people, but the sophistication of the technology, low levels of trust, and the avowed motives of some key tech actors all point to a culture of manipulation in the online world.   This manipulation may occur as a result of market players' own business strategies, or as a result of the activities of individuals and states seeking to disrupt or subvert markets and public opinion through the use of platform intermediaries.    According to a 201 8  Reporting, a researcher gained access to the personal data of millions of Facebook users through a third-party app he developed on Facebook and shared the data with data analysis company Cambridge Analytica.  This Data  was allegedly used to identify and profile voters in order to target them with political messages.   In 2017, the main platforms admitted that ‘divisive' material – advertisements and messages from fake accounts – had reached over 125 million users in the United States. Online manipulation is  seen as  too systematic, too existential in its challenge to human rights, to be solved by  companies .   However  Self-regulation  has  an  crucial role to play .  Social media platforms have a crucial role on protection of their users. Due to protect EU citizens and their personal data, GDPR allows only limited targeting methods, requiring user consent even to limited data. However, even in the  cases where data controllers have the consent of users to get tailored messages they still have limited criteria for targeting messages.  One imported key is that this consent always has to be given freely, specific, and informed.  Also it is important to be separated from the acceptation of general terms and privacy policy of platform. By imposing fundamental rights-friendly standards for how individuals and organizations can send personalized messaging to the public (such as political messages) and limiting microtargeting, the right to access  information  is protected, as is free dom of  expression and political d ebate . Chapter 3. Legal regulation of social media: challenges and practice 3.1. The role of international human rights law in regulating social media platforms 3.1.1. Restrictions of the operation and access to social media platforms The foremost definition concept in relation to freedom of expression is endorsed by Article 19 of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights  (UDHR)  and Article 19 of the International Covenant and Political Rights  (ICCPR) . According to Article 19 of the UDHR: “ Everyone has the right to freedom of opinion and expression; this right includes freedom to hold opinions without interference and to seek, receive and impart information and ideas through any media and regardless of frontiers ” This clause includes the first and generally recognized argument on the right to freedom of expression.  The UN Human Rights Committee provided interpretation of Article 19 of ICCPR in General Comments, with the most authoritative being  provided in 2011 in General Comment 34 (UNHRC/GC34).  The Committee has explained on the meaning of the right to freedom of expression, and required States parties to ICCPR to mull over the importance for freedom of expression of improvements in ICTs, such as the Web and mobile-based electronic data spread systems. The Human Rights Council unanimously adopted in 2012 the landmark Resolution on the promotion, protection and enjoyment of human rights on the Internet, expressing that the same rights that individuals have offline must also be secured online, in specific freedom of expression, which is appropriate regardless of frontiers and through any media of one ’ s choice in accordance with Articles 19 UDHR and ICCPR. Article 10 of  ECHR  provides that everyone has the right to freedom of expression, including freedom to hold opinions and to receive and impart information and ideas without interference. Be that as it may, these freedoms may be subject to limitations.  In any case of initiating the restriction of the right of expression online, the latter must conform with article 19(3) of International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights. Specifically, they must demonstrate that the limitation is: Provided by law, so it must be clearly formulated in law in order to give individuals the opportunity to regulate behavior in accordance with the circumstances. In pursuit of a legitimate aim, such as respect for the rights or reputations of others or the protection of national security and public order, or public health or morals. Necessary in a democratic society, requiring the State to demonstrate in a specific and individualized way the precise nature of the threat to a legitimate aim, and the necessity and proportionality of the specific action taken, in  particular by establishing a direct and immediate between the expression and the threat identified.   Moreover, the Strasbourg Court has developed similar standard basic tests to be applied to these restrictions, which must: be based on “law”, that is on legal rules that meet quality requirements of clarity, accessibility and foreseeability; serve a legitimate purpose in such a society, that is a “pressing social need”; be “necessary” to achieve that purpose, that is they must not be disproportionate to the purpose, nor ineffective; have an "effective remedy", preferably judicial, if they do not meet these tests. However, some states not always comply restrictions within the international law standards of such limitations. Freedom House based in a study, cites that restrictions of websites are increasing.  This study took place in 37 countries and mention that authoritarian states in attempts to reduce political opposition have started to apply “measures” by blocking and filtering websites, punishment of ordinary users, content manipulation, or coercion of website owners to remove content.  It appears that Internet restrictions around the world are partially a reaction to the increasing popularity of sites like Facebook or Twitter, as well as their prominent role in political and social activism. In 12 of the countries surveyed, including Egypt and Tunisia, where democratic activists strongly relied on Facebook to mobilize supporters and organize mass demonstrations, governments regularly or briefly shut down those pages.  These actions are taken most of the time on an  unclear grounds  in lack of domestic  laws.  The decision about limitation or shouting down these platforms is rarely taken by an independent court or adjudicatory authorities. Blocking of content/sites or censorship by pressure are some of measures governments use to interfere with internet freedoms and their restrictions. The main goal of blocking is to stop a specific online content to reach the final user through software or hardware that examines contents and determines whether or not to block reception based on pre-determined standards.  It has little impact on the content that is being targeted. This means that the content itself is not removed from the internet but only blocked on specific platform. A common case can be blocking images of child abuse. It is thought that a more effective way would be to remove the images at all from internet and to criminally investigate producers.  This issue arises in human rights law when determining if a legal standard is effective and thus "proportional."   Blocking is a broad complex idea, and not all forms of it are similarly effective or lawful. It may sounds that it is very easy but the capabilities of technologies are complex and often can be circumvented. In many cases may occur sites without prohibited contents being blocked or vice versa (cites with prohibited contents slip through a filter). In an Open Society Institute study, all blocking technologies were found to have flaws in terms of over- or under-blocking.
