Freedom House identified arbitrariness and opacity concerning content blocking decisions in all of the countries studied: “in most non­democratic environments, there is no government initiative to tell the public whose content is blocked and why.”  Authorities also refuse to announce that a website has been banned, preferring either to stay quiet or citing technical issues. Even in more open, democratic environments, censorship decisions  are often taken by private companies without public debate, and complaints mechanisms are also onerous, little known, or non-existent. What is considered blocked material may expand without transparency. Sites containing not only hate speech and terrorist promotion, but also political dialogue, material on minority rights, alleged defamation, and also "sacred texts" have been blocked.   Censorship by pressure occurs when governments authorities contact authors or platforms to apply pressure for specific contents to be removed, threatening legal action, contract or license cancellation, and outright bans – even if the companies are based in other countries. Also government supporters complain (encouraged from government) to platforms about content. Following such complaints, YouTube and Facebook have removed or disabled activist accounts in China, Mexico, Tunisia and Ethiopia.  These pressures raise human rights issue, including whether companies should be required to resist coercion in order to protect their users' human rights. The four special rapporteurs in 2011 Joint Declaration has discussed the problematic nature of these measures, where it was stated that: a.  Mandatory blocking of entire websites, IP addresses, ports, network protocols or types of uses (such as social networking) is an extreme measure - analogous to banning a newspaper or broadcaster - which can only be justified in accordance with international standards, for example where necessary to protect children against sexual abuse. b.  Content filtering systems which are imposed by a government or commercial service provider and which are not end-user controlled are a form of prior censorship and are not justifiable as a restriction on freedom of expression. c.  Products designed to facilitate end-user filtering should be required to be accompanied by clear information to end-users about how they work and their potential pitfalls in terms of over-inclusive filtering. 3.1.2. A human right to access to social media platforms? Social media, as demonstrated earlier has been perceived as a threat to many governments,  authoritarian and quasi-authoritarian  but even liberal democratic ones. When governments want to eliminate free expression and the likelihood of revolt, they often start by shutting down social media, rather than the entire Internet. Examples of such practice included the cases of Tunisia, China or Mexico where platforms faced restrictions by the governments. These actions raise a number of questions regarding whether there are circumstances when governments can legitimately block social media platforms, and whether users (citizens) should have an unconditional right to use these platforms in all circumstances. If the right to access these platforms is determined to be so, what are the grounds for recognizing these services as a right? Access to the Internet in general and to the social media in particular is very important for the enjoyment of the right to freedom of expression and other rights in the digital age. It has been found that in the online world, the right to freedom of expression and the media freedom become pointless without the means to communicate or an affordable access.  Social media is not technically considered as a public forum since the private Internet Service Provider (ISP) provides the basic means of access and responsible for the control of the content are private companies.  In the case when users (individuals or entities) have been restricted ( exmpl . filtered content) or denied access in platforms purposefully or interrupted by government intervention, right to freedom of expression and other rights are engaged.  It cannot be said the same in the case when these restrictions and deniable are done from the private companies. However this distinction is still unclear, this because of the fact that many social media platforms cooperate with governments by  providing  large  quantities of personal knowledge to government authorities. The right to universal internet access has been discussed or referred to in many documents, despite the fact that it has not yet been established as a distinct human right under international law.  Some   examples   of   these   documents   are :   The Declaration of Principles of the 2003 World Summit on the Information Society (WSIS) states that “communication is a fundamental social process, a basic human need and the foundation of all social organization. It is central to the Information Society. Everyone, everywhere should have the opportunity to participate and no one should be excluded from the benefits the Information Society offers.” The 2011 report of the UN Special Rapporteur on freedom of expression called on states "to ensure that internet access is maintained at all times, including during times of political unrest."  it was also mentioned that access to the infrastructure and “ensuring universal access to the internet should be a priority for all States. Each State should thus develop a concrete and effective policy, in consultation with individuals from all sections of society, including the private sector and relevant Government ministries, to make the internet widely available, accessible and affordable to all segments of the population.” The 2011 Joint Declaration of four special mandates on freedom of expression stressed that “states are under a positive obligation to facilitate universal access to the internet” and that “giving effect to the right to freedom of expression imposes an obligation on States to promote universal access to the internet.” Access to the internet is recognized in some national legislation as a basic human right or as an implied element of the universal right to freedom of expression. The acknowledgment of social media as a human right is distinctive because many important rights are link up to it, such as freedom of expression, information or freedom of association, right to family life or private life. Users of platforms have chosen to join them and interact with other users (friends, followers). Most of the platforms give the opportunity to their users to create their groups and become members of them and interact with other members without being needed to be “friends”. Therefore every user can follow and become member of groups they belong. Many informal and typically formal groups are now social media-based, with participants interacting with each other solely or in accordance with their social media accounts. Although not all people participate in such digital platforms, for those who do, participation is critical to everyday life, and being denied access can be damaging. People lose a significant associative privilege if such access is restricted or terminated, even for a short time. But it isn't the only thing they lose. Well-known expressive and informative components remain vital to social media, especially when these sites are based on conversation and information, the ability to engage in conversation and have access to the information the citizen wants to follow is fundamental, these rights are strongly respected offline, and they should  be no different in the digital world. Article 19(2) of ICCPR includes the right to information, where provides that “Everyone shall have the right to freedom of expression; this right shall include freedom to seek, receive and impart information and ideas of all kinds, regardless of frontiers, either orally, in writing or in print, in the form of art, or through any other media of his choice.”  Seeking, receiving and imparting information are main components of social, as they are to the daily offline social life. Considering the nature of social networks in relation to the importance it has for users in consuming some of the main fundamental rights protected by international human rights law. If this access to social networks is prohibited, there would be a violation of the right to freedom of expression, protected by international human rights law as mentioned  mentioned  above. Banning access to social networks would also not be a mere restriction of a channel which provides the opportunity to express and receive information. More than that should be seen as a limitation of a social environment where many users have established relationships with each other, in the same way as they are established in real life. Hence, a ban on access to social media networks would have the same consequences as an unjustified restriction on privacy or freedom of association (art 22 ICCPR). Therefore, International human rights law although not directly it again protects access to social media networks through the rights deriving from it, such as freedom of expression, information or freedom of association, right to family life or private life. 3.2. Private law vs. public international law Last years there has been an increase of calls worldwide including EU for greater regulation of social media platforms. This has come as a result of several factors. As noted, social networks hold considerable power over the flow of information online and everything published on the platforms of these companies. As  mentioned earlier in the online world of social networks there are a series of problems occurring regarding the content published by different users and their interaction. The way digital content management issues are handled by digital companies has been of concern to many stakeholders such as governments, lawmakers, civil society, regulators, self-regulatory institutions, media and the public also. To define whether an online content on social networks is harmful and illegal requires to be analyzed in complexity and to consider a number of factors which include in-depth knowledge with the consideration of illegal content taking into account the local and regional context in the economic-political, social and cultural dimension, as well as strong knowledge of international human rights standards and relevant legal issues. As mentioned earlier, At the international level international standards recognize that the same rights that people have offline must be recognized and protected online, also restrictions in any case must be justified on the basis of the same criteria as in the offline world.  So in any case of initiating the restriction of the right of expression, the latter must conform to the strict requirements of the three-part test. Specifically, they must demonstrate that the limitation is: - Provided by law, so it must be clearly formulated in law in order to give individuals the opportunity to regulate behavior in accordance with the circumstances. - In pursuit of a legitimate aim, such as respect for the rights or reputations of others or the protection of national security and public order, or public health or morals. - Necessary in a democratic society, requiring the State to demonstrate in a specific and individualized way the precise nature of the threat to a legitimate aim, and the necessity and proportionality of the specific action taken, in particular by establishing a direct and immediate between the expression and the threat identified. When the  right to freedom of expression is restricted, less restrictive measures should be imposed capable of achieving a given legitimate objective. Moreover, as it is provided in a rticle 20(2) of the ICCPR ,  States are  required  to prohibit by law “any advocacy of national, racial or religious hatred that constitutes incitement to discrimination, hostility or violence . In the debates regarding the regulation of content on social networks, it has been continuously requested that these platforms be treated in the same way as traditional media, as another form of publication. In general, regarding the regulation of the Media, there are some examples of regulatory systems where it can be mentioned the  statuory  regulation, where this model applies every measure passed by the parliament to regulate the media, is characterized by strong state intervention. Another model is the co-regulation model, otherwise known as "regulated self-regulation". This model has an element of self-regulation supported by legislation. The legislative approach is seen as prone to producing vague legal concepts that can be abused, as well as disproportionate penalties that promote unnecessary censorship in online world. Eventually, legislation is power tools in the field of social media, unable to easily respond to the ever-changing circumstances of platform content publication and dissemination. As a result, a regulatory system that can cope with the multitude of problems that the world of social media presents is needed. The most preferred model for print media is the self-regulation model, which is totally based on voluntary compliance. Legislation plays no role in implementing the relevant standards. Its aim is to hold its members accountable to the public, to promote knowledge among members, and to develop and respect ethical standards.  The self-regulation model is considered preferred for print media also because it is less restrictive, whereby it is possible to promote higher standards in the media. Also, an effective self-regulatory mechanism can reduce pressure on the judiciary. This is because many issues can be resolved more quickly and effectively, at a much lower cost. Generally, when a problem is effectively solved through self-regulation, the need for state regulation is eliminated. These proposals for legal regulation suggest that they be considered more carefully, always in accordance with international standards of the right to freedom of expression. Based on this, is needed to recall that international standards of freedom of expression differentiate the type of media regulation based on the type and characteristics of the media. An important question that arises from this is whether social media is just another type of media? Social Media is considered to be different from other forms of freedom of expression. Unlike traditional media which aim to produce content, expression on social media networks is not just another way of publishing but rather it is considered another way of interaction. Its function is based more on hosting, so providing space for individuals and companies to make their content available to the public; also online dissemination and only in special and limited cases some social networks have the role of traditional media.  Exploring and adapting existing models of social media self-regulation based on ethical standards and effectively adapting to the online world is believed to provide a better and more effective solution. The current application of various self-regulatory models of social media networks in some cases has made positive steps in some aspects, but in order to be as more effective, still require improvements, both in relation to international standards of freedom of expression and not only. The  2017 German  Network Enforcement Act   ( NetzDG ), One of the most popular legislative initiative taken on content moderation  make s  reference to some form of self-regulation in relation to social media .  NetzDG  also allows the Ministry of Justice to recognize "regulated self-regulatory agencies”. Their role is to determine whether content is in compliance with the law and in case of unlawful content to be removed from the platform. The Ministry of Justice's recognition is conditional on the self-regulatory agency's integrity as an independent one, the competence of agency personnel who will serve as decision-makers, and the agency's ability to take a decision within seven days. This model is might be seen as a positive step towards creating an effective self-regulatory system for social media platforms. However, there are arguments which find the guarantees of this model as not enough to ensure  independence and effectiveness for a successful self-regulation system and for the protection of freedom of expression. Self-regulatory models are also mentioned in voluntary processes between digital companies and different public bodies resolving "hate speech" and other topics, such as the EU Code of Conduct on Hate Speech. However, it is seen that they are in contravention of international human rights law. They use ambiguous and overbroad language to describe illegal material, assign censorship duties to social media platforms without taking into account the legality of the content, and refuse to offer due process protections. 3.3.  Conflict of jurisdictions in the context of operation of social media platforms   Social networks nowadays is an international phenomenon and among other things their transnational operation makes them major players worldwide in upholding fundamental human rights such as the right to freedom of expression. In order to ensure corporate respect for human rights, governments must provide an effective policy and regulatory framework that encourages business respect for human rights and responsibility for negative consequences.  Under international human rights law, states are obligated to protect people against human rights violations perpetrated by  private actors , including businesses.   Companies are obeyed to the laws of the countries in which they operate, However, there may be situations where a state lacks the institutional capacity or is unwilling to impose national laws and regulations against corporations, especially transnational corporations as dominant social media networks are. Currently, international human rights law does not explicitly require states to regulate the extraterritorial operations of corporations based in their territories and/or  jurisdiction.  They are also not generally prohibited from doing so where a known jurisdictional basis exists. Under these frameworks, several human rights treaty bodies have recommended that home states take action to prevent business entities within their jurisdictions from abusing human rights abroad.  In conflict-affected countries, the risk of gross human rights violations is highest. The "host" state may not be able or willing to safeguard human rights in such regions, and there may be no state power or authority over the area. In such cases, "home" states play a critical role in offering counsel, support, and guidance, as well as requiring companies operating in these countries to ensure that they are not engaged in human rights violations. Most worldwide dominant social media networks like Facebook or Twitter which operate worldwide have their headquarters in USA. However, the way how they operate is not the same in all the countries they have presence. Situations of restriction of freedom of expression on social networks under the influence of pressure from the authorities of several countries have been increasing in recent years. Also, social networks in most cases have not made any kind of resistance to the protection of users' human rights. Moreover, since social media network companies like Facebook or Twitter are incorporated in the USA, it ensures that the United states has an obligation for ensuring that Facebook complies with international human rights laws everywhere it operates, including outside of US jurisdiction. Therefore it means that these digital companies have to be unable to impose restrictions on freedom of expression. In this respect, states, in different sectors of businesses have taken a variety of approaches. Some of the steps are domestic with extraterritorial implications.  Multilateral soft-law instruments such as the  Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises of the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development ; and provisions for  "parent" firms to report on the global operation of the entire company are only a few examples.   Furthermore,  The Human Rights Committee, the expert committee on the International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights, has  mentioned , in its concluding observations to State s  parties, that a responsibility to set out clearly the expectation that companies domiciled in their territory or jurisdiction should respect human rights  during  their operations can be inferred from the human rights obligations under the Covenant. The Committee has also urged States to ensure that people whose human rights have been violated by certain companies operating abroad have access to remedy. (CCPR/C/DEU/CO/6) Arguably, application of international human rights obligations of the host state to the activities of the social media companies outside of the territory of their domicile can be based on the models developed in international jurisprudence, such as the “effects doctrine”, or a general duty of a state to avoid harmful activities (violations of internationally recognized human rights) generating from their territory. In this respect the state of incorporation has a limited positive obligation to ensure that social media companies comply with core human rights standards. 3.4 .  Self-governance (corporate regulations) vs. legal regulations 3. 4 .1. Legal obligations of social media platforms towards users Terms of Service, which are established and enforced directly by service providers, regulate the contractual arrangement between online platforms and their users. Users must agree with the company's terms of use in order to access the online platform (mostly are provided with such statement like “I have read and accept the terms of services”), which define dispute resolution jurisdictions and reserve  authority regarding content and account activities.  These provisions normally followed by other documentation including privacy policies, cookie policies, data policy and community standards . Platform content policies are a subset of such concepts that define the limitations of what users can express and how they can express it.  Most companies do not specifically focus their eligibility criteria on any body of law that can regulate expression, such as national or international human rights law. “Terms of service” are basically uniform contracts between users and platforms where user has no negotiation option but only to accept or reject it. Once a user agrees the terms, users as well as digital platforms are obligated to follow what was agreed upon.  The most relevant obligations of social media platforms towards users aim on the protection of freedom of expression and privacy. The maintain of freedom of expression from companies in their platforms is mostly based on their content moderation which consist in monitoring, blocking, filtering or removing contents which do not comply with the terms. For example In the case of Facebook, These contents may consist in:  Content  t hat violates these Terms, Community Standards and other terms and policies that apply to users use of Facebook. Content  t hat is unlawful, misleading, discriminatory or fraudulent. Content  t hat infringes or violates someone else's rights, including their intellectual property rights.  Also in cases when users  may  u pload viruses or malicious code or do anything that could disable, overburden, or impair the proper working or appearance of  Facebook’s   products. However, there are cases where the moderation of content is done for undetermined or unclear reasons.  International organisations have expressed their opinion over online platform Terms of Service. Such terms, according to the UN Special Rapporteur on Freedom of Opinion and Expression, should be transparent and consistent with international human rights standards.  In comparison, the Council of Europe's Guide to Human Rights for Internet Users reaffirms that human rights prevail any terms and conditions put on Internet users by any private agent, and emphasizes that states must support, protect, and encourage human rights, as well as supervise the private sector.  Talking about the right to privacy, various jurisdictions have followed a model based on the concept of privacy self-management, in which data subjects are responsible for deciding whether or not to allow the collection of their personal data, taking into account the costs and benefits. Mechanisms for reporting abusive content that is deemed abusive by users seem to be receiving further attention. This in some situations, it may help users' privileges be better protected and more effective than legal regulation may be. It is however recommended to be combined with effective protections of freedom of expression so that it does not serve as an indirect reward for indiscriminate content removal. In  Delfi  vs Estonia  The European Court of Human Rights shows its approach regarding social media platforms obligation on content-moderation regarding freedom of speech also their liability for what their users share. Despite the fact that the offensive remarks were removed after  Delfi  was placed on notice, the Estonian Supreme Court ruled that the online news platform  Delfi  was responsible for discriminatory and threatening user generated content (UCG) posted on its site. The Grand Chamber has concluded that the Estonian courts' finding of liability against  Delfi  was a justified and proportionate infringement on the news portal's freedom of  speech, in particular because the posts in question were extreme and posted in response to an article published by  Delfi  on its professionally controlled and professionally operated news portal.  Moreover,  Delfi's  actions to delete the derogatory messages as soon as they were published were insufficient, and the 320 euro damages award that  Delfi  was required to pay to the complainant were considered as not excessive for  Delfi , one of Estonia's main internet portals. 3. 4 .2. Legal obligations of social media platforms towards states In principle, each company agrees to abide by the laws of the jurisdiction in which it operates. The platform of Facebook states: “If, after careful legal review, we determine that the content is illegal under local law, then we make it unavailable in the relevant country or territory.   Social media platforms frequently are facing state requirements to restrict clearly unlawful content, such as representations of child sexual abuse, direct and credible threats of injury, incitement to violence, assuming that they meet the legitimacy and necessity.   Recent years there is an increase of countries where social media platforms are being obligated toward state to monitor and rapidly remove user generated  contet .  Putting in place punitive mechanisms it is seen likely to stifle freedom of expression even in democratic societies. Under German Network enforcement act ( NetzDG ), platforms operating in Germany are obligated to remove rapidly content inconsistent with the local law otherwise will be faced with penalties.  The European Commission has also recommended that member states establish legislative  obligations for active content monitoring and filtering.  Guidelines on the dissemination of social media online content during elections introduced in Kenya in 2017 require social media platforms to “shut down accounts used in disseminating undesirable political content on their networks” within 24 hours. Social media platforms being under such a pressure of penalties for removing the unlawful content rapidly may contribute also negatively to freedom of expression by risking it. Regarding states requiring platforms to remove unlawful content, the latter ones act limited in territory when the request is done based on the law removals of the state (it is applied only under the jurisdiction of the country) but when the request is done based on the terms of services the removal then is been applied globally.  Therefore, there is an increase of state authorities to require content removal outside legal process.  The European Union and four major social media companies (Facebook, Microsoft, Twitter and You Tube) agreed to remove content under the 2016 European Union Code of Conduct on fighting illegal hate speech online, pledging them to cooperate with "trusted flaggers" and support "neutral counter-narratives." (In the course of 2018, Instagram, Snapchat and Dailymotion joined the Code of Conduct. Jeuxvideo.com joined in January 2019, and  TikTok  announced their participation to the Code in September 2020.)  Although this mechanism may be attractive regarding extremist contents, such approach may runs the risk of turning platforms into propaganda vehicles well outside existing areas of legitimate concern. When relevant State legislation is unclear, subject to differing interpretations, or in conflict with human rights law, committing to legal enforcement can be  difficult. For example, regulations against "extremism," which leave the main word vague, give government officials flexibility to press digital companies to remove content on dubious grounds.  Similarly, companies are often pressured to comply with state legislation that criminalize content that is blasphemous,  criticising  the government, defamatory of public officials, or fake. T here is a positive duty on the state to protect users’ freedom of expression on S ocial  Media . In  Beizaras and Levickas v. Lithuania  t he European Court of Human Rights has found that  comments tha t  amount to hate speech and incitement to violence, and are thus clearly unlawful on their face, may in principle require the States to take certain positive measures. [para 125] Two men posted a photo of a kiss on social media in 2014. The picture generated homophobic comments intended to stir up hatred. The men approached the Lithuanian Gay League (LGL), which reported the incident to the Prosecutor General's Office. The criminal code of Lithuania, specifically article 170  prohibits incitement to hatred or discrimination based on sexual orientation . However, The Office of the Prosecutor General did not initiate a pre-trial investigation into the case by arguing that the users  had acted “unethically” but that such “amoral behaviour” had not reached the threshold required by Article 170 §§ 2 and 3 of the Criminal Code . The ruling was overturned on appeal, but the court has decided that no pre-trial review could be carried out.  The Court recall ed  its finding that comments that amount to hate speech and incitement to violence, and are thus clearly unlawful on their face, may in principle require the States to take certain positive measures . It has also been held that inciting hate does not always imply inciting abuse or other violent acts. Attacks on individuals perpetrated by insulting, humiliating, or slandering specific groups of the community may be enough for authorities to favor combating racial speech in the face of irresponsible exercise of freedom of expression. 3. 5 . Is (international) law capable of regulating social media? I t is essential to recognize the importance of social media companies in the modern world.  The rise of social networking platforms has changed the modern contact scenario dramatically.  These digital companies now play a significant role in how an increasing part of the world's population interacts with it. For some people, they serve as an essential channel and source of information. Last years it has been obvious that their role worldwide in public sphere is significant. Individuals' rights to freedom of expression online are thus heavily affected and dictated by the activities of social media.  Analyzing the nature and characteristics of social media, concluded that the expression on social media platforms is not just another way of publication, but it is a qualitatively different way of interaction where its function is based more on hosting, providing space for individuals and companies to make their content available to the public; online dissemination; and only in special and limited cases some social networks have the role of traditional media. Another question which has been raised is whether  there is a human right to access to social media platforms which can be derived from the right to freedom of expression and the right to private and family life.  Access to the internet in general and to the social media in particular is very important for the enjoyment of the right to freedom of expression and other rights in the digital age. It has been found that in the online world, the right to freedom of expression and the media freedom become pointless without the means to communicate or an affordable access.    Considering the variety of activities (and their nature) users have while participating in digital platforms,  makes this participation  critical to everyday life, and being denied access can be damaging. People lose a significant associative privilege if such access is restricted or terminated, even for a short time.   Regulation of social media platforms is a challenging task where law as a regulatory tool has a limited capacity. Law is seen as prone to producing vague legal  concepts that can be abused, as well as disproportionate penalties that promote unnecessary censorship in online world. Eventually, legislation is power tools in the field of social media, unable to easily respond to the ever-changing circumstances of platform content publication and dissemination. As a result, a regulatory system that can cope with the multitude of problems that the world of social media presents is needed.  Self-regulation (corporate governance) is seen more effective for the operation of social media platforms. This model is seen as more preferable because of numerus factors. It is less restrictive,  whereby it is possible to promote higher standards. Moreover, an effective self-regulatory mechanism can reduce pressure on the judiciary. It happens because many issues can be resolved more quickly and effectively, at a much lower cost. Generally, when a problem is effectively solved through self-regulation, the need for state regulation is eliminated. The self-regulation model is considered preferred also because it is less restrictive, whereby it is possible to promote higher standards in the media. These proposals for legal regulation suggest that they can be considered more carefully, always in accordance with international standards of the right to freedom of expression. The current application of various self-regulatory models of social media networks in some cases has made positive steps in some aspects, but in order to be more effective and to give more guarantee, still require improvements in relation to international standards of freedom of expression. Exploring and adapting existing models of social media self-regulation based on ethical standards and effectively applying them to the online world is believed to provide a better and more effective solution. International human rights law applies to social media platforms in many interrelated ways and provides important tools for balancing the interests of social networks, their users, and society at large, as well as those of the state . While most social media platforms are private business corporations aimed at generating income, there is a growing perception of them as environments, which are becoming less “virtual” as the difference between virtual and real interaction becomes less obvious. Looking at the social media networks as not only tools  providing venues for expression but rather as virtual societies is essential for understanding special dimensions of applicability of existing legal concepts for regulating social networks and for appreciating the limitations of such legal concepts. Summary of findings Legal restrictions on the operation of social media platforms have been addressed by scholars, human rights  organisations  and international institutions as an interference with a freedom of expression, that is a freedom to hold opinions and disseminate information. It has been demonstrated in the present research that on many instances freedom of expression on social media goes beyond this classic political freedom and is closer related to private and family life. This dimension of the freedom of expression encompasses essential characteristics of the person’s identity, his or her ability to relate to other people, including family members, and develop own personality and professional connections. A person’s ‘virtual’ identity developed with the use of social media needs to be treated on the same basis as his or her identity in the off-line life. Accordingly, any interference with freedom of expression must be balanced not only against the traditional criteria established under Article 19 of the ICCPR or Article 10 of the ECHR, but equally against the right to private or family life. Law has important limitations for effective regulation of social media platforms. First, given the nature of Internet in general and the social networks in particular, protection of privacy Second, the dynamics of user-generated content on the social media makes it virtually impossible to monitor and delete unlawful information. Third, definition of unlawfulness of user-generated contents depends to the greater extent on domestic regulations and to some extent on international standards. It is thus impractical and contrary to the nature of social networks to impose legal obligations to regulate free speech by technical means. The emergence of artificial intelligence-based technics for moderating contents on social networks raises additional important concerns over unjust interference with fundamental human rights. It is thus argued that  the responsibility  of compliance with legal standards relating to free speech, on the same basis is  the responsibility of protecting personal privacy shifts to individuals  rather than on social media companies. There is an emerging tendency in international law to recognize the positive obligation of states to protect freedom of expression on social networks from  interferences by private parties. This obligation is limited to establishing a regulatory framework providing for legal duties on the part of social media operators, and conducting effective investigation of offences committed on social networks on the same ground as offline. To this extent this obligation can be characterized as ‘minimum core obligation’ under international human rights law. By the same token, there is an emerging recognition of states’ extraterritorial obligations to ensure compliance of social media corporations with international human rights obligations of the state of domicile. However, international human rights law as it stands now does not establish clear requirements for regulating and the scope of such obligations depends on varying approaches of domestic jurisdictions. At the same time effective protection of the right to a freedom of expression on social networks in the jurisdictions immune to existing human rights monitoring procedures can be indirectly achieved through the application of free trade guarantees, such as the WTO regime.
